speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number four with Sonia Friedman. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we went back to Russia. And when we came to Russia, this man that was my director already made papers for my whole family that it was an end for this man, my-- our friend, and for his wife. He left two sons. OK, I won't get into it because-- so when we traveled back, they worked in the same organization where you-- I worked in the office, and my father worked in the warehouse, and so did-- and we lived together in the place where the office was, in same building. We had apartments. I think maybe same apartment, really. And in our house, of all the people that I knew, didn't see anybody. They told me a close friend of mine was in the partisans. She's somewhere in the woods. I met one friend of my brother. Now, meanwhile, my parents were frantically looking what happened to my brother. They only wrote-- they already sent letters to the Red Cross and to the [INAUDIBLE], and they told them that the school was bombarded heavily when they were on maneuvers in a town called [INAUDIBLE]. And as far as they know, there are no survivors. But they put notes all over, and I meet this friend of my brother. He was-- he was studying in France in Annecy. He was a very prominent family. He was there with his mother and father. I don't even know how he survived. I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I, all the time, keep-- I suspect that everybody working for the KGB-- I couldn't make close contact with anybody. Everybody, I thought, is-- everybody got close to me, I thought is working for the KGB, because one time, the same man that helped me approached me. He wanted to send me to school. Says, 'You want to take revenge on the Germans?' And I mean, by nature, I'm a terrible coward. That's the truth. I wear my emotions on my face. Believe me, I can't even say a lie, not only be a, a spy.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he was nice. He probably didn't press. Maybe this was the idea from the beginning, to get me involved. But-- so I suspected everybody. So he was-- I mean, this was the only person I really knew in town that I knew before. But I was constantly on guard with everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then we decided to move out, to go farther to Poland. This man that took me on the truck, my boss, came drunk to my house, and he said Sonja, you don't know what you're doing. We'll be there. Stop running. You're just-- you could-- you could advance very good. Don't go. I mean our house was like, why was I so afraid of the KGB? Our house was full.

speaker: interviewee
text: Those two people that send me the shoes, they were very community-oriented people. They were wealthy people before the war, and she was a, a society woman. She was a philanthropist, and our house was a stop place for all the people that came out from the partisans from Vilna, all the people that later we saw their faces, they came to our house, and they stayed with us. I had to go and buy them tickets to leave. I used to look Russian. All the time I tried not to look Polish when I was in Russia. I didn't want to dress-- I, I, I tried to look Russian. Anyway, they used to send me, and each time I thought I'll never see my parents again or my family, because they'll arrest me. I made so many times papers. Now, they used our office stamp. Another director-- he was a young boy who survived from Limberg. A dynamite-- I mean, a guy who would put a-- he jumped out from going trains. He went through-- he had a stamp, and he freely gave it to all the Jewish organizations that had to transport people to make some legal papers. So the house was really a den of conspiracy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Each time-- and we had lot of military Russians that were helping out-- officers-- and each time they, they saw a party in the house the militia will knock on the door. And they say I'm getting engaged. And so who is their [INAUDIBLE], so they show the military men. So the next time he comes again, and they tell the same thing. So he says I'm not a guide. He says, what happened? If fell through with the other guide. And they were helping a lot, the Jewish military.

speaker: interviewee
text: We still didn't know about concentration camps and anything like that. We-- on a December night in the cold, we packed everything, whatever we could carry with us, and, yeah, we came back to the house. And they arrested my father, and they said if you ever step in again, you'll never see your children again, and that was the end with that house. It was-- the why-- he was working for [INAUDIBLE] there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were-- we crossed the border, and we came to Chelm. And Chelm was a godforsaken place. We were there six weeks. There was another Jewish writer. His name was-- I forgot. He was with [INAUDIBLE]. There were a whole group of people. There was no heating. We were cold and hungry, and we were waiting for the Germans to go farther. When the Germans left Lublin, we came to Lublin.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, we left in December, and, and, Pesach-- for passover we arrived in Lodz. The Germans just left. The people, those that were hiding, came in and took away the German officers', which were Jewish to start with, apartments. And there was a curfew, and we were invited. The whole group there was invited to a house were there were three sons and a mother. Very Orthodox Jew-- my first look at Orthodox young people, because the rabbi in our city was wearing one of the [NON-ENGLISH]. And I have not seen very Orthodox people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we stay there all night, and we see an album. Those boys are wearing-- we were three girls. There were three boys about a little bit older than we were, and they were all wearing payots. And we were hysterical. We were so laughing at night, because we never saw them. And those are the boys that were there. There was another neighbor, a Jewish boy with his mother that came out, and in the end, we went back to Lublin and tried to make papers so we can leave Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents came-- I wasn't with them-- and we brought the papers. And we left Poland. And they left, too, that family. And they-- I mean, they had, from the Germans, I mean, silver, and porcelain, and art, and they had all kind of-- but they were rich people before. They were very Orthodox and rich people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went to Czechoslovakia, and that's when-- yeah, when I was in Lublin-- in Lodz, actually, I saw in the building lived a blonde woman, real bleached blonde. She was Jewish, and she had a boyfriend looking like a prize fighter. She wasn't cons-- she was on Irish papers, and he was in the Sonderkommando [INAUDIBLE]. And that's where I started to hear the first stories.

speaker: interviewee
text: They both didn't look Jewish. He looked like-- didn't look Jewish. He looked like-- and I-- and then, when we started to travel, on every station we see those emaciated [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] faces and bodies with the [INAUDIBLE] in the stripes in the delousing stations, like they were spraying powder. And we start hearing these horror stories that happened. I mean, we-- I don't know if I mentioned at the beginning. We suffered terrible hunger in this village the nine months. I mean excruciating hunger. Going back to it, we were sitting all in the evening and put a big pot of water, and put in maybe a few spoons of flour, and cook in it. And there-- the family of five. My father looked, at that time, why I got back to it like from concentration camp, because he was a very-- he was like a Polish officer-- straight, tall. He was a very strong-looking man, and he had a hollow face with sunken eyes. Because I remember he wouldn't take more of the food than the children. And he was-- we were all starving there. I mean, later there were bad times, but we didn't have that actual starvation. There was nowhere to take it. There was nobody who wanted to give, and there was nobody to sell. We used to get enough food for about 10 days and 30 days. Like, we'd get enough flour to make bread and maybe some potatoes dug out, sometimes half frozen.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when I started to see those people, they looked all like that. They looked all like that, and we used to see a lot of groups when we came. And we came to Czechoslovakia, and we stayed for six weeks. And it was a beautiful place. It was just right after [INAUDIBLE]. Where did you go after Czechoslovakia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where we go from Czechoslovakia? We wanted to go to Romania and go to Israel, but when we came to Slovakia, they told us that you can't go. You can't go there anymore. Now, a lot of our people wanted to go on the American side. We didn't know where we want to go. My mother got sick in between, and we were just drifting from one place to another one. We went to Hungary. We meet more people in-- like, you're going with a group. I don't even remember this period. We were in Bratislava, and then we were in Budapest. From Budapest, we learned about [INAUDIBLE], about groups of Israelis that helping out. We went to Austria. In Austria, they divided us, the whole family, because we get to. And I have a phobia. I am afraid of heights. So they didn't-- they divide-- took away all the pictures we had, everything, no baggage, and everything, and divided us separately so if anybody gets stopped or arrested that the rest doesn't know, and it does not hold back. And I remember the heights now and alone, because nobody was with me. And that's when they took everything away, and we crossed the Austrian border. And we came to Graz, and I told you before informally about that hotel and the little boy that was with us because we picked him up in Lodz, the little boy that didn't have anybody. And he was traveling with us. And there we met the Israeli [INAUDIBLE] in [INAUDIBLE], on the border of Italy and Austria, and it seemed like an idyllic place. But they told us don't even think that this is what it is. It's just a rest for three days. From here, you go to Italy. And it was in the middle of the summer already, and the heat was unbearable. The Italians were all leaving for vacation for [? Fiere ?]. And there-- and we were driving-- traveling with open trucks, I remember, and the sun was burning. And Italy was beautiful, and the waters.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what year did you meet your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: '45. That was '46. No, I met my husband '46. This is '45, and we didn't know. We didn't have a home. We didn't have-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] we stopped, like, in a makeshift area with tents that the Israeli [INAUDIBLE]. And there were like. Showers. You could then take a shower there. And I went with a towel to take a shower. When I come back, I see my mother is walk-- talking to one of those Israeli officers. I come close. He laughs, and he says, I would recognize her with the upturned nose. We knew each other, were children. He went to Israel from this small town, Wisniowiec.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he says to us, you know, you'll have a hard time. Stay here with our unit for a while. You'll have a good time. I said, we're going with a transport. Where will we go? I mean, we don't know where we're going now. Where are we going to look? He said, I promise you. We'll take you to join your group. So-- Sonia, when did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got married in '46.

speaker: interviewee
text: I met my husband in the end of May when I found my brother. I was going to the border of Italy and Austria. And I met my husband, and we got married in October the 20th.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you come to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came to America in 1948.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in February.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was your child born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1949 in November.

speaker: interviewer
text: In America?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what city?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was born in New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long have you lived in Los Angeles?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was three years in New York, and 42 years-- 42 years in California.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old is your daughter today?

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughter is 45.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does she do?

speaker: interviewee
text: She's a professor. She has a PhD in psychol-- in educational psychology. She's on the board of directors of the [INAUDIBLE] Temple and of a lot of organizations.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where does she live?

speaker: interviewee
text: She lives in Malibu right next to the Getty Museum.

speaker: interviewer
text: Does she have any children?

speaker: interviewee
text: She has two children.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are there names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kevin and Kelly.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old are they?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kevin is 10-- 11 and a half. And Kelly is nine and a half.

speaker: interviewer
text: From your years of being persecuted and having to run away as a Jew, have you learned anything that you want to pass on to future generations, to your, your daughter, to your grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: I always told them what my mother told me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Everything in life can be taken away from you, except what you got here-- what you got here. And that's what I always taught my daughter. She sent me a card for Mother's Day, which reflects what I always taught her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] The picture of my father, Perez Epstein, was taken in Florence, Italy, in 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- how old was he when he died?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was 92 years old when he died.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Los Angeles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is a picture of my mother, Chaia Epstein. The picture was taken in Florence in 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did she die?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother died in '78. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] And this picture is of Sonia Friedman. At that time, it was Sonia Epstein. It was taken in Florence in 1945. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This picture is of my brother, [INAUDIBLE] Epstein, with a friend. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was taken in Stuttgart, Germany [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in 1945 or 1946. I'm not sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother was lost all during the war. He was in, in a military school. He was a prisoner of war in Russia in Minsk for a long time. They didn't know he was Jewish. He had an infection, angina, and he didn't go to a doctor because he was afraid they'll discover that he's a Jew. Later he was freed by the partisans, but from-- and he was with the Russian advanced army to Germany. He was a tank commander, and he had endocarditis lenta, an inflammation of the heart muscle, that came from the-- originally from the infection that was not treated. At that time, penicillin was scarce, even in Italy. And after we got-- we were united, he lived six weeks, and he died. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This picture is of Sonia and Sam Friedman one day after they got married in 1946 in Florence, Italy. This picture is of my sister-- my younger sister, Rosa. Used to be Epstein, now it's [? Halperin ?]. She's a pediatrician. She got her medical degree in Vienna, Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture was taken when she gave birth to her first son.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was born--

speaker: interviewee
text: --in Detroit. She was a resident there. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This picture was taken in the house of my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Los Angeles. My parents, Perez and Chaia Epstein. My daughter is-- was 12 years old. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That's '71.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Keep it running.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, this 17-- '78.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Picture is my daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Esther Friedman, now Sinclair, and her husband is Keith Sinclair.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did the wedding take place?

speaker: interviewee
text: The wedding-- the wedding took place in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Los Angeles, the Friars Club, in 19--

speaker: interviewer
text: And talk.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of Kevin and Kelly Sinclair, my two grandchildren. The picture was taken in their house.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Los Angeles?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Pacific Palisades. And Kevin is here about [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 10 and a half or 11. And Kelly is two years younger. Now Kevin is 11 and a half, and Kelly is 9 and a half.
